text:
I have been relatively quiet this week in the midst of such volatility and with very good reason because I am on a break in China (my very maiden trip here).

What I have seen and experienced so far related to energy:

1️⃣ High speed train reaching a speed of 348 km/h from Beijing to Tianjin, electric powered, no less

2️⃣ Busy roads and highways, quieter than most because of influx of EV vehicles. But it doesn't mean no traditional transport fuelled cars though.

3️⃣ Lots of English words everywhere but not much spoken (good luck with ordering food in a random shop!)

4️⃣ Amazing skyscrapers and construction in many places. Tianjin in particular surprised me with vibrant lights and laser show on the facade of multiple buildings 

5️⃣ A strong sense of culture steeped in thousands of years of history yet striving for innovation and hunger for success

Can't say I have witnessed demand destruction first hand.

Photo taken at Temple of Heaven where I heard some Americans talking to a local about Elon Musk being there recently. I was thinking of Trump more as I walked up those steps!

Drinking from the cup of poison: White House reluctantly catches up with the oil market

Oil traders and hedge funds increasingly anticipated the imminent re-opening of the Strait of Hormuz for more than a month before the United States and Iran concluded a memorandum of understanding ending their war on June 15.

With petroleum inventories at home running down rapidly, top U.S. policymakers faced an increasingly urgent deadline to decide whether to reach an agreement with Iran or attempt to re-open the Strait fully by force. 

Ultimately, the White House seems to have decided the embarrassment of a diplomatic deal achieving less than its stated war aims was a lesser evil than the potential risks of trying to force the Strait open against hostile fire. 

In recent weeks, the threats, blockades, clandestine convoys and attacks on missile and drone facilities near the waterway can all be seen as an effort to escape making this unpleasant choice. 

But after exploring various options, the White House appears to have concluded none of the military ones was acceptable and it would have to settle for a deal achieving much less than it initially hoped when the war started.  

In reaching that decision, the White House was catching up with a conclusion many oil traders and fund managers had reached weeks earlier ...

Earlier this morning, Pakistan's National Hydrographic Office reported a confirmed mine sighting in the southern Omani transit lane through Hormuz.

Bloomberg: The mine was reported just off the northwest tip of the Musandam peninsula, the Omani exclave that protrudes into the strait. All vessels transiting through the area are advised to navigate with extreme caution, the notice said.

The cost of shipping #containers continues to surge, with the Drewry Composite Index rising 12% over the past week, extending its seven-week gain to nearly 80%. Freight rates on the key Shanghai–New York and Shanghai–Rotterdam routes have almost doubled during that period, driven by a "perfect storm" of overlapping geopolitical, regulatory, and seasonal supply chain pressures.

In essence, a surge that isn't driven by a boom in global consumer spending, but rather an artificial rush of buying for vessel space to outrun tariffs, regulatory deadlines, and extended transit times at a time of elevated fuel costs. 

The index has risen 79% YTD and 21% over the past twelve months.

Maritime traffic via the Strait of Hormuz will only return to normal levels when mines are removed and traditional routes restored, some of the world's largest shipping organizations said June 18 while calling for more clarity on transit rules and coordination among authorities to allow safe passage. https://t.co/xouak9yKnr

Soybean Futures Weekly Outlook - June 15-18, 2026

The grain futures market was closed today, Friday, June 19, 2026, in observance of the Juneteenth federal holiday. Because CBOT and CME markets sat dark with no open trading or new settlement prices disseminated, the week's trading wrapped up at Thursday's close.

The update has been adjusted to reflect the final positions from Thursday afternoon.

Top News Story: Peace Framework Flashes Hammer Energy; Renewed Export Volume Cushions the Board

The soybean complex negotiated a highly volatile, holiday-shortened trading week, fighting off heavy macro headwinds to establish a provisional floor. The dominant market driver shifted rapidly from domestic weather to global energy channels following headlines of a tentative U.S.-Iran peace framework aimed at reopening the Strait of Hormuz. The geopolitical breakthrough sent WTI crude oil plunging to three-month lows near $74 per barrel, dragging the soybean oil sub-complex down to a two-month low.

However, the board managed to avoid a complete structural breakdown thanks to a sudden resurgence in export demand. Private exporters confirmed daily flash sales of 132,000 metric tons of soybeans to China and an additional 120,000 metric tons to unknown destinations for the 2026/27 marketing year. This return of international buying interest, combined with pre-holiday short-covering, allowed July futures to bounce off its weekly lows and settle at $11.22 6/8 on Thursday before the Juneteenth closure.

Market Notes

Crop development continues at an aggressive, uninterrupted pace across the U.S. heartland, minimizing seasonal risk premium. Monday's USDA Crop Progress report highlighted that national planting is effectively entering the rearview mirror at 95% complete.

The USDA's updated national condition ratings ticked upward by one point to 66% good-to-excellent, recovering the ground lost during the previous week's heavy rains. Favorable, mild temperatures and localized showers across the Corn Belt over the past week have proved structurally supportive for early vegetative growth.

Locally in Nebraska, fields are showing high emergence parity with minimal replant required. While topsoil moisture profiles remain a focal point for late-summer pod setting, the immediate canopy look is excellent. Domestic processing demand remains an aggressive consumer of spot physical bushels, insulating regional basis lines from the broader crude-oil-led collapse in vegetable oil values.

Watchlist Performance

Trading concluded for the week at Thursday afternoon's close due to the Friday trading holiday. Performance tracking from Monday Open:

ContractLast (Thu Close)Day Change (Thu)Since Monday Open

July 2026 (ZSN26)$11.22 6/8-$0.08 4/0+$0.03 6/0
August 2026 (ZSQ26)$11.26 2/0-$0.08 2/0+$0.07 4/0
September 2026 (ZSU26)$11.19 4/0-$0.07 2/0+$0.07 2/0
November 2026 (ZSX26)$11.39 0/0-$0.07 0/0+$0.07 0/0

Trend: Despite an 8-cent pullback during Thursday's session, the entire futures complex logged minor net gains for the shortened week. The nearby July contract found solid footing above its previous four-month low, while the new-crop November maturity added a modest 7 cents over the four-day trading window. 

Cash Trade: The DTN National Soybean Index mirrored the board's consolidation, stabilizing near the $10.70 marker as country elevators kept basis steady ahead of the long holiday weekend.Technical Note: July futures traded a definitive range between the 10-day and 200-day moving averages ($11.20 and $11.38, respectively). The June 15 low of $11.02 1/2 has solidified as a major double-bottom support corridor.

Key Market Factors
Supply Constraints: Planting completion at 95% shifts all attention toward early-summer weather patterns. Extreme systemic acreage disruptions are no longer on the table, keeping speculative bulls on a tight leash until the June 30 Acreage report provides hard infrastructure data.

Demand Drivers: China’s return to the physical market provided an essential psychological lift. The 132,000 metric ton flash sale hints that lower spot pricing is finally triggering international value procurement, balancing out a sluggish old-crop export pace.

External Influences: The broader macro landscape remains intensely hostile to commodity length. The U.S. Dollar Index (DXY) marched to fresh one-year highs near 100.7 following hawkish shifts in Federal Reserve policy guidance, severely restricting runaway upside for dollar-denominated ag sectors.

Technicals: Rigid floor support sits at $11.02 to $11.10 on the July tape. Overhead resistance remains clustered at the 20-day moving average near $11.48 3/4.

Upcoming USDA Reports Expectations
The holiday closure shortens the preparation window for the blockbuster June 30 Acreage and Quarterly Grain Stocks report. When trading resumes on Monday, desks will immediately begin balancing portfolios ahead of this data, while simultaneously dissecting the June 22 Crop Progress updates to gauge whether the national good-to-excellent rating can hold its 66% baseline.

Funds Activity
Managed money participants decelerated their aggressive short-building program this week. The combination of verified daily export sales and pre-holiday risk-mitigation forced automated trend-followers to buy back a portion of their nearby short exposure. However, non-commercial funds retain a firm net-short posture, treating this week's minor technical bounce as a corrective pause rather than a true trend reversal.

Outlook for the Week Ahead
When the electronic boards re-open Sunday evening, the market will instantly react to any shifts in the weekend weather models. If the 7-to-15 day maps continue to print consistent moisture and mild temperatures across the Western Corn Belt, the export-driven momentum will likely fade, returning the focus to a heavy supply outlook. Expect choppy, range-bound trade as the market steps into the shadow of the major June 30 acreage adjustments.

Key Events: 

Weekly Crop Progress & Condition Ratings (June 22); Weekly Export Sales (June 25); Position squaring ahead of the June 30 Acreage Report.

Strategy: For operations holding remaining old-crop inventory, the mid-week recovery toward $11.30 July provided a welcome exit window. Do not gamble on a late-summer logistical squeeze if macro financial currents remain defensive. For new-crop positions, maintain resting defensive orders on November futures at $11.50 to catch automated spikes if extended dry weather runs manifest in early July.

text:
By 8:40 a.m. Eastern Time today, oil had reached $79.46 per barrel, measured using the Brent benchmark. That’s $1.32 less than it cost yesterday morning and about $3.13 above its price a year earlier.

Will oil prices go up?

Oil prices are inherently unpredictable. While many variables come into play, the basic push and pull of supply and demand is what ultimately matters. In times of heightened concern about recession, war, or other major disruptions, oil can swing suddenly.

How oil prices translate to gas pump prices

Each gallon you pay for at the pump bundles together several costs. Crude oil is one piece, but you also pay for refineries, wholesalers, government taxes, and the price markup set by gas stations.

Because crude oil usually accounts for more than half of the price per gallon, it tends to move the needle the most. Sharp increases in oil almost always show up quickly at the pump. Declines in the price of oil, on the other hand, often translate into slower, more delayed drops in gas prices—the “rockets and feathers” effect.

The role of the U.S. Strategic Petroleum Reserve

When an emergency arises, the U.S. has a reserve of crude oil called the Strategic Petroleum Reserve. Its chief function is to secure energy during disasters like sanctions, severe storm damage, or war. It can also help take the edge off brutal price spikes when supply gets hit.

It’s not a solution for the long haul. It’s more of an immediate safety net to support consumers and keep crucial sectors of the economy running (think key industries, emergency services, public transportation, and the like).

How oil and natural gas prices are linked

Oil and natural gas are two of the main fuels that keep the world running. A big change in oil prices can end up affecting natural gas. As an example, if oil prices increase, some industries may sub natural gas for certain areas of their operations wherever possible. This can increase demand for natural gas.

Historical performance of oil

The oil market typically tracks two benchmarks:

  • Brent crude oil (the main global oil benchmark)
  • West Texas Intermediate (WTI) (the main benchmark of North America)

Between the two, Brent offers a clearer view of global oil performance because it prices much of the world’s traded crude. It’s also often the preferred gauge for tracking historical oil trends. In fact, the U.S. Energy Information Administration now uses Brent as its primary reference in its Annual Energy Outlook.

Looking at the Brent benchmark over multiple decades, you’ll find oil has been anything but stable. It’s seen sharp rises due to factors like wars and supply cuts, along with steep declines tied to global recessions and oversupply (called a “glut”). For example:

  • The early 1970s saw the first major oil shock when the Middle East slashed exports and placed an embargo on the U.S. and others during the Yom Kippur War.
  • Prices fell in the mid-1980s for reasons including lower demand and the entry of more non-OPEC oil producers.
  • Prices jumped again in 2008 with increased global demand, but then plunged alongside the global financial crisis.
  • During the 2020 COVID lockdown, oil demand collapsed like never before—bringing prices below $20 per barrel.

Bottom line, oil’s historical performance has been anything but smooth. It’s hugely affected by wars, recessions, OPEC whims, evolving energy initiatives and policies, and much more.

Energy coverage from Fortune

Looking to stay up-to-date regarding the latest energy developments? Check out our recent coverage:

  • The G7 just pledged to break China’s rare earth grip
  • The Iran war exposed Southeast Asia’s energy vulnerability
  • Filling up your car won’t feel normal until next summer, S&P says

Frequently asked questions

How is the current price of oil per barrel actually determined?

The current price of oil per barrel depends largely on supply and demand, including news about potential future supply and demand (geopolitics, decisions made by OPEC+, etc.). In the U.S., prices also move based on how friendly an administration is to drilling, as it can affect future supply. For example, 2025 saw the Trump administration move to reopen more than 1.5 million acres in the Coastal Plain of the Arctic National Wildlife Refuge for oil and gas leasing, reversing the Biden administration’s policy of limiting oil drilling in the Arctic.

How often does the price of oil change during the day?

The price of oil updates constantly when the “futures” markets are open. A futures market is effectively an auction where people agree to buy or sell oil in the future. As long as people and companies are trading contracts, the oil price is changing.

How does U.S. shale oil production affect the current price of oil?

In short, shale is rock that contains oil and natural gas. Think of shale as energy yet to be tapped. The more shale the U.S. accesses, the more energy we’ll have—and the more easily oil prices can keep from spiking as much thanks to a greater supply.

How does the current price of oil impact inflation and the broader economy?

When oil is expensive, it tends to make everyday items cost more. This can be related to energy (your heating, gas utilities, etc.), but it’s also due to the logistics involved with making those items accessible to you. Shipping, for example, can affect the price of things at the grocery store, as it’s more expensive to get those products from warehouses and farms onto the shelf.

This story was originally featured on https://t.co/opgY7EYkBs

Nebraska Cash Grain Markets
Friday, June 19, 2026

Grain markets across Nebraska faced a truncated and volatile trading week, closing out early on Thursday afternoon ahead of the Friday federal holiday. Heavy late-week fund liquidation hit the row-crop complex hard as macroeconomic pressures led by the U.S. dollar index surging to a one-year high overshadowed a string of private export sales to China and Mexico. Local elevator bids remained highly defensive as commercial buyers maintained wide basis margins against the volatile board action.

Beyond row crop progress, extreme seasonal weather patterns are causing a deep fracture between livestock and small grain outlooks across the state. A catastrophic combination of multi-year drought and late-spring weather has decimated the regional winter wheat crop, with the USDA rating a staggering 84% of Nebraska's wheat in poor to very poor condition. This intense dryness has similarly withered pastures leaving 79% of the state's range and pasture land in poor to very poor shape and forcing a wave of early cattle liquidations across western counties as ranchers run out of viable forage heading into June.

In the livestock markets, June Live Cattle were up 0.25 to $242.00. Feeders were down from $264.00 to $261.00. And, Lean Hogs were up $3 to $99.00.

Behind the Market Movement
The primary driver behind this week's shifting price action was a sharp mid-week short-covering rally that ran into macroeconomic roadblocks by Thursday's close. Soybeans initially surged to a two-week high on rumors of aggressive Chinese buying, which the USDA later confirmed with flash sales totaling 4.9 million bushels. 

However, a post-Federal Reserve rally pushed the U.S. dollar to a one-year high, making domestic grain expensive abroad and stopping the rally in its tracks. Meanwhile, crude oil plunged to its lowest levels since the start of the Iran conflict following an interim deal to secure the Strait of Hormuz, dragging down the biofuel complex. Wheat managed to hold onto sharp double-digit gains on the week as the market reacted to escalating security risks and port attacks in the Black Sea grain corridor.

Tip for Sellers
With old crop storage bins needing to be cleared before wheat harvest, focus heavily on the short-covering spurts. The board carry into December new crop corn remains respectable near the $4.44 mark. Consider locking in incremental new crop corn bushels if the December contract retests technical resistance at $4.50, rather than waiting out midsummer weather lotteries.

Weekly Market Outlook
Looking ahead to next week, traders will be hyper-focused on the upcoming USDA Acreage report on June 30 and updated crop condition ratings. Analysts expect a drop in corn condition scores due to excessive, flooding rainfall in parts of the eastern Corn Belt contrasted against the stubborn drought bands remaining in the West. 

Keep a close eye on the $11.10 mark for July soybeans; a breach below this level will likely prompt local elevators to drop cash bids to protect their margins before transport lines tighten.

This is the official story from the US Government, the EIA:

US Commercial Crude Oil Inventories were drawn down by -8.26m barrels for the week ending June 12th.  This brought US Commercial Crude inventories to 418.2m barrels.

The average draw over the last four weeks is -6.71m.

If we assume a weekly draw of 6.71m barrels for the week ending today, June 19th, that would bring commercial inventories down to 411.5m barrels.

The operational floor is said to be around 350m barrels.  Oil in pipelines can’t be drained, the pipelines require hydraulic pressure.  Sludge in the bottom of tanks, etc.

This leaves US Commercial Crude Inventories at just 61.5m barrels.  With an average draw of -6.7m barrels per week, the US has just nine weeks left.

Iran just closed the Strait again.  JD Vance has cancelled his trip to Switzerland to sign a peace agreement with Iran.  The clock is ticking.  Brent short interest is at a 5-year high.

At what point do the markets panic?  8 weeks?  7 weeks?  6 weeks?  It could be any day now.  Cushing was at the operational minimum inventory level of 20m barrels as of June 12th.

text:
US crude inventories are at their lowest since 1985, yet WTI timespreads are not signalling the tightness you might expect.   

In the week ending 12 June, US crude stocks drew by 8.3 mb, taking outright levels to their lowest since 1985. Cushing fell to levels near operational minimums, and PADD 2 stocks hit their lowest since 2014. But the latest 40 mb SPR exchange, higher Permian crude inflows to Cushing and the announcement of a US–Iran MoU are all working to ease the near-term supply picture.   

Across products, the MoU is already shifting trade economics: jet export demand from the Gulf Coast is expected to slow, while Asian LPG buyers have adopted a wait-and-see stance ahead of potential freight rate easing through Hormuz. Our latest US Oil Weekly breaks down the draw across all the barrels.   

Read more: https://t.co/byGRebrgDK

#US #CrudeOil #Inventories 🛢️⛽️

📍US commercial crude oil inventories are reaching critical levels:

Crude inventories at Cushing, Oklahoma, the largest commercial storage hub in the US and the pricing point for WTI Crude, dropped -1.6M 🛢️ last week, to 20M 🛢️, (..) https://t.co/MY2ylY7YUP

Cushing, Oklahoma crude inventories have fallen to their lowest level since October 2014 according to the latest data.

Eight consecutive weekly draws have pushed stocks just above the roughly 20 million barrel level widely viewed as the hub’s functional “tank bottom.” Cushing is a key U.S. oil storage hub and serves as the physical settlement point for West Texas Intermediate futures.

While total capacity is near 90 million barrels, headline storage figures may overstate usable supply, as a portion of remaining crude can include sludge or material that is difficult to move operationally.

The drawdown is part of a broader decline in U.S. crude inventories. The Department of Energy report showed a national crude drawdown of 8.26 million barrels, compared with a consensus estimate of 3.5 million barrels.

Source: The Deep Dive
